ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for lychee-berry flavoured carbonated soda specifically; FOODON_03301710 (carbonated beverage) used as the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKM (Carbonated soft drinks) is the closest defensible category; no specific lychee-berry soda subcategory exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for lychee mixed berry soda; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass serving of approximately 350 g, as typically served at a café or bubble tea outlet in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Beverages, carbonated, cola, regular (FDC ID 174851) and Lychee, raw (FDC ID 167765); values adapted to represent a lychee-berry syrup soda at approximately 10 g sugar per 100 g
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB entry exists for a lychee mixed berry soda with 10 g sugar per 100 g. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for carbonated soft drinks and raw lychee. The input per_100g values (GrabFood menu listing, AI-generated, confidence 0.7) are retained as they are internally consistent and align with approved-source analogues: energy 42.86 kcal, protein 0.14 g, fat 0.03 g, carbohydrate 10.86 g, sugar 10.0 g, sodium 5.71 mg, dietary fibre 0.29 g, cholesterol 0.0 mg, saturated fat 0.0 g, trans fat 0.0 g, vitamin C 4.29 mg. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, calcium, iron, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are set to -1 as detailed micronutrient data for this specific commercial preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Soft drink, carbonated, average (GI 63); University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for sugar-sweetened carbonated soft drink.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lychee mixed berry soda. GI 63 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sugar-sweetened carbonated soft drinks (average GI ~63, medium category), which is the closest defensible analogous food. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI x carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (63 x 38.01) / 100 = 23.95, rounded to 23.9. Carbohydrate per serving = 10.86 g per 100 g x 3.5 (350 g serving) = 38.01 g. Serving size used: 350 g (1 glass).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (10.0 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention, despite very low sodium (5.71 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit sugar-sweetened beverages such as this lychee berry soda, as the 10 g sugar per 100 g load independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; an unsweetened version using fresh fruit and plain carbonated water is a suitable alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 10.0 g sugar per 100 g (38.5 g per 350 g serving) from lychee and berry syrups; liquid sugar causes rapid glucose absorption with minimal fibre buffering (0.29 g per 100 g).
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high sugar content (10 g per 100 g, approximately 38.5 g per serving) and medium GI of 63, resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 24 per serving; the ADA classifies sugar-sweetened beverages as a primary dietary risk factor for glycaemic dysregulation and recommends avoidance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar from syrups (10 g per 100 g) contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and elevated cardiovascular risk; fat and saturated fat are negligible (0.03 g and 0.0 g per 100 g respectively).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of this sugar-sweetened beverage is inadvisable for individuals with cardiovascular disease, as the high added sugar load (10 g per 100 g) is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines; fat and sodium content are low and do not independently pose cardiovascular concern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory sugar load (10 g per 100 g) from syrups overrides the anti-inflammatory anthocyanin and vitamin C benefits of the mixed berries.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage in its syrup-sweetened form; requesting a version with fresh berries, no syrups, and plain carbonated water would preserve the anti-inflammatory anthocyanin and vitamin C benefits without the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iced Lychee Mixed Berries Soda is a chilled, carbonated fruit-flavoured beverage popular in Singaporean cafés and bubble tea outlets. It is prepared by combining lychee-flavoured syrup and mixed berry syrup (typically incorporating blueberry, raspberry, and strawberry flavours) with carbonated water and served over ice in a tall glass. The drink has a sweet, floral lychee aroma layered with tart berry notes, a light effervescent texture, and a refreshing cold finish. It is a non-alcoholic, vegan, and gluten-free beverage. Nutritionally, it is a sugar-sweetened beverage providing approximately 42.86 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates as the dominant macronutrient (10.86 g per 100 g, of which 10.0 g is sugar). Protein (0.14 g) and fat (0.03 g) are negligible trace amounts. Sodium is very low at 5.71 mg per 100 g. Vitamin C is present at 4.29 mg per 100 g, contributed by the berry components. A standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 150 kcal and 38.5 g of sugar, making it a high-sugar beverage by clinical standards. The glycemic index is estimated at 63 (medium) based on analogous sugar-sweetened carbonated soft drink data from the University of Sydney GI Database, with a glycemic load of approximately 24 per serving. This beverage is classified as unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and should be consumed with caution by those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its high added sugar content. The mixed berry components contribute anthocyanins and vitamin C, which have anti-inflammatory properties, but these benefits are outweighed by the syrup-derived sugar load in the standard preparation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in iced lychee soda?
Can diabetics drink lychee mixed berry soda?
Is lychee soda suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of lychee berry soda?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for lychee mixed berry soda exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database. Per-100g nutrient values are retained from the input record (GrabFood menu listing, AI-generated, confidence 0.7) and are consistent with analogous fruit-flavoured carbonated soft drinks in USDA SR Legacy. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry (sugar-sweetened carbonated soft drink, University of Sydney GI Database). Confidence is therefore medium.
